text: Root of inferior mesenteric artery bleeding during robotic D, three lymph node dissection for rectal cancer, a video vignette. This is a seventy-one-year-old man who presented with hematochesia for three months. Coloscopy showed a mass in upper rectum. Biopsy revealed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. No distant metastasis.

The patient is positioned supine with legs apart on the operating table, which is set in a Trendelenburg position with a right lateral inclination. The surgeon uses eight-millimeter trocars and one twelve-millimeter assistant trocar to mobilize the sigmoid colon from the left abdominal wall, followed by mobilization of the sigmoid mesentery via a medial approach.

The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin. An improper dissection with a permanent cautery hook leads to vascular avulsion at the artery's root. Compression hemostasis is applied using fenestrated bipolar forceps. The cautery hook is replaced with a needle driver, and a figure-eight suture combined with a running suture is performed on the bleeding site using a 4-0 Prolene suture.
The surgeon reinforces the previously sutured bleeding site with an additional 4-0 Prolene suture, ensuring hemostasis as no active bleeding is observed.

Then, TME and end-to-end colorectal anastomosis were performed conventionally. The operation time was three hours and twenty minutes. The estimated blood loss was one hundred milliliters. The patient was discharged on postoperative day seven.

There are some tips for the procedure. Tip one, permanent cautery hook should not be used for the transection of major vessels because of the potential thermal conduction. Tip two, in the situation of IMA root injury, use the compression to control the bleeding immediately. Tip three, robotic repair of major vascular injuries with proline suturing could be attempted by experienced surgeons. In conclusion, bleeding at root of IMA may be managed safely in robotic setting, thereby avoiding conversion to laparotomy. Thank you for your attention.
